text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 10 February 2023 

Dr Fiona  
Centre for Epilepsy  
Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Fiona,                                                                                                         

James Carter, DOB: 22-11-1990, Hospital No: H563920 NHS No. 8475623901  
Flat 4, 28 King Street, London, W6 0QP  

Epilepsy 

Medication: Discontinued Levetiracetam January 2023; no current regular antiseizure medication. 

Levetiracetam: previously 500 mg twice daily, self-tapered and stopped on 10 January 2023 after discussion with local services regarding side effects (reported low mood and irritability). 

Lamotrigine: discussed historically as a potential alternative but never initiated. No other antiseizure therapies currently prescribed. 

Blood Tests/Drug Levels: None indicated presently as not on a maintenance antiseizure therapy. Routine safety bloods with GP last month reportedly within normal limits (patient-reported). 

Seizures: He came off Levetiracetam on 10 January. In the following week, he had 2 - 3 seizures, one associated with sleep deprivation. No further seizures have occurred since. He denies injuries or prolonged post-ictal confusion. Partner reports brief loss of awareness with tonic stiffening and tongue bite on one occasion; recovery within 15 minutes. No auras described. 

Background: History of childhood febrile seizures noted; no neurological sequelae. Family history negative for epilepsy. No history of significant head injury, CNS infection, or substance misuse. Currently employed; reports improved mood since stopping Levetiracetam. Driving status: not driving and aware of DVLA requirements following recent seizures. 

Assessment: Interval cluster of seizures temporally related to cessation of Levetiracetam, with subsequent stability. Given the current seizure-free period since mid-January and absence of red flags, he prefers to remain off medication while maintaining close monitoring and lifestyle measures. We discussed sleep regularity, alcohol moderation, and adherence to safety precautions. 

Plan: 
- No immediate initiation of antiseizure medication, in line with patient preference; to reassess if any further events occur. 
- Seizure diary to continue; partner to document witnessed features and durations. 
- Safety counselling provided regarding showers (avoid baths), heights, swimming with supervision only, and machinery use. 
- DVLA guidance reiterated; he understands to inform authorities and not to drive until appropriately cleared. 
- Arrange routine EEG and MRI review of prior imaging to ensure no interval concerns; will update if abnormalities are identified. 
- Telephone review in 8 weeks, sooner if recurrent events. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Clinic date is 10 Feb 2023. He stopped Levetiracetam on 10 Jan 2023. In the week following 10 Jan, he had 2–3 seizures; after that, no further seizures occurred up to the clinic date (about 1 month later). He is not seizure-free for ≥6 months, so a seizure-free label is not applicable. Over the ~1-month interval before review, total seizures = 2–3, yielding a current frequency of 2 to 3 per month.
